text: Hello, good morning. This is a case of mini gastric bypass.
This patient has undergone the sleeve gastrectomy approximately five to seven years ago and there was forbis ring also applied.
But in spite of that, after a few years, he started gaining weight. And again he wants weight reduction and we are doing the mini gastric bypass.
The surgeon observes a slightly fatty liver with adhesions between the left lobe of the liver and the phobic string.
The surgeon performs adhesiolysis to inspect the phobic string, checking its position and functionality within the surgical field.

The atraumatic grasper holds a small band of adhesion, with the blue lock of the ring visible on the right side and the white ring visible. The surgeon uses a harmonic device to gently cut the adhesion, freeing the liver from the ring.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel in the right hand to perform surgical actions, likely involving cutting or dissection.
The surgeon uses an atraumatic grasper to gently remove adhesions from the left lower area, gradually freeing the tissue.

So the sleeve gastrectomy in especially the carbohydrate eaters or grazers doesn't work that much good. And Roux-en-Y gastric bypass has a lot of malnutrition problem. So mini gastric bypass is very good in this group of the patient which has a promising result.
The surgeon completes the removal of the adhesion, freeing the previously adhered structures.
And it's working nicely.
The surgeon attempts to insert a gastric calibration tube into the patient's stomach, but the tube cannot pass due to the presence of a fauvist ring obstructing its path.
The surgeon begins freeing the omentum from the lesser curvature of the stomach, approximately twelve centimeters below the fauvist ring, to create a retro gastric tunnel.
The surgeon uses a harmonic device to slowly separate the omentum from the lesser curvature of the stomach, creating a visible tunnel and forming a clear window for the gastric pouch.

The surgeon assesses the window created in the lesser curvature, ensuring it is sufficient for using the endo-GI linear stapler to divide the stomach. The greater curvature is visible, showing a sleeve stomach configuration. The surgeon plans to create a gastric pouch and perform a side-to-side anastomosis using the endo-GI linear stapler.
So why? Because already phobias ring is there and that is already offering the restrictive part of the obesity control. So we need only bypass part.
The surgeon demonstrates the retro-gastric tunnel, lifting the stomach like a bread to position it above the pancreas, ensuring clear separation from underlying structures.

The surgeon carefully lifts the stomach with the left hand to avoid injury to the omental bursa or pancreas, using a harmonic device to create a retrogastric tunnel. The stomach is gradually separated, with minor bleeding being coagulated, as the tunnel formation progresses.
The surgeon carefully positions the silicon jaw of the harmonic scalpel towards the stomach to prevent accidental perforation of the posterior wall, while slowly separating the tissue.

The surgeon observes the formation of a window through the retrogastric tunnel, with the liver visible through the tunnel as the gastric pouch is prepared for side-to-side anastomosis using the endo-GI linear stapler.

The surgeon enlarges the retrogastric tunnel slightly to accommodate the endo GI stapler jaw comfortably. The grasper is positioned over the anterior surface of the left lobe of the liver, clearly identifying the anatomical structures involved.

The surgeon uses an Endo GIA linear stapler with a blue cartridge, a Covidien Tri-Stapler, to divide the dilated portion of the stomach. The tip of the stapler is visible as it fires, but additional cartridges are needed to complete the division due to the sleeve gastrectomy configuration.
The surgeon uses an additional cartridge to fire the stapler, completing the division of the stomach. The stapler is fired again to ensure complete separation.
The surgeon has completed the creation of the gastric pouch, which is now visibly formed and ready for the next step in the gastric bypass procedure.
The surgeon brings the jejunostomy intestinal loop into view, positioning it near the elongated gastric pouch.
We have kept it long because already far base ring is doing the job and it should be better to have it long so that you can have the less complication. And now this is fine.

The surgeon identifies the secondary gastric pedicle and pushes the omentum upwards towards the transverse colon. After lifting the omentum, the transverse colon is visible. The surgeon then lifts the transverse colon further to locate the start of the jejunum, pulling the intestinal loop down if it moves upwards.
The surgeon pulls down the small intestine while lifting the omentum to locate the beginning of the jejunum, identifying anatomical landmarks in the process.

The surgeon identifies the jejunum and the inferior mesenteric vein, along with the ligamentum teres. The surgeon then measures the jejunum in increments of five centimeters, counting up to twenty-six increments to achieve a total length of 130 centimeters for the malabsorptive component of the procedure.
The surgeon counts and measures the length of the jejunum, ensuring there is no torsion by spreading the intestinal loop smoothly.

The surgeon counts the jejunal length to 250 centimeters, marking the site for anastomosis. The jejunum is brought up, and an 8mm opening is created in both the gastric pouch and small intestine using a harmonic scalpel to prepare for the gastrojejunostomy.
The surgeon perforates the gastric pouch, ensuring the mucosa is visible to avoid creating a sub-serosal tunnel.
The surgeon attempts to insert graspers into the lumen of the gastric pouch. The gastric bougie, which aids in perforation, cannot be introduced due to a four-way string obstruction.
The lumen of the intestine and the lumen of the stomach are clearly visible, indicating readiness for anastomosis using an endostapler or hand-sewn technique.

The surgeon creates a small perforation in the anti-mesenteric border of the intestine, using the vibrating jaw of the harmonic to carefully enlarge the incision. Horizontal holding and anterior traction are applied to prevent the lumen from touching the opposite mucosa, achieving the opening in the intestine for the gastrointestinal anastomosis.

The surgeon confirms the lumen by introducing a grasper, then uses a tri-stapler to perform the anastomosis. The face jaw of the stapler is positioned in the intestine, while the cartilage jaw is placed in the stomach. The jaws are aligned parallel to each other for controlled stapling, and the assistant aids in holding them together before firing the stapler to achieve a mini gastric bypass with an end-to-side anastomosis.

But here again I am telling you, we don't want any distinctive part because already we have forbis ring in the place. Generally the ring, when it is used in the sleeve gastrectomy, generally there is no failure. But I don't know why in this patient, in spite of that, he has weight regain. So mini gastric bypass is planned and it is a better option.
The surgeon completes the side-to-side anastomosis between the stomach and intestine. The internal suture line is inspected, showing no signs of bleeding.
The surgeon begins suturing at the corners of the anastomosis site, ensuring the junction between the intestinal and gastric lumens is secure.

The surgeon sutures the corners using a 20-centimeter bi-directional barb suture with a readymade loop, applying an out-to-in and in-to-out technique to achieve full thickness closure in a single layer.

The surgeon uses a barb suture with a pre-tied loop to perform anastomosis, pulling the loop through the tissue. If using vicryl, a dandy jamming knot is applied for continuous suturing and aberrant termination.

The surgeon uses a barb suture to initiate the closure, demonstrating how the suture locks itself without the need for additional knots. The suture is pulled through, sliding and locking into place as a starter knot.

The surgeon uses an open jaw grasper to tighten the pre-tied starter knot, which is already locked. The loop becomes smaller as the knot is secured. The surgeon continues suturing from out to in and in to out, progressively narrowing the side-to-side anastomosis.

The surgeon performs full-thickness suturing of the gastric pouch, ensuring the closure is airtight. After suturing, a leak test is conducted using 150 ml of methylene blue dye injected into the gastric pouch and jejunum, both clamped with atraumatic graspers, to check for any leaks.

The surgeon begins the closure of the gastric pouch using a barb suture, performing layer-by-layer suturing with a two-third curve needle. Care is taken to avoid excessive traction on the needle. The lesser omentum is visible attached to the gastric loop, providing support as the closure progresses. The previously opened stapler line is being sutured.

The surgeon uses a barb suture with laser cuts every one millimeter to secure the tissue, demonstrating how it holds and hangs the tissue more effectively than normal Vicryl sutures. The barb suture tightens automatically upon pulling, eliminating the need for knots even at termination.

The surgeon performs continuous suturing using a polygalactane barb suture, which has laser cuts to hook onto the tissue and prevent slipping. This is the final stage of closure in the mini gastric bypass procedure, ensuring double security with the knots.

The surgeon uses two needle holders to tighten the sutures, ensuring they do not override each other. The open jaw of the grasper is used to pull and tighten the sutures, providing support. The grasper is preferred for holding the tissue within the lumen due to its atraumatic nature, while the needle holder is used for handling the needle.

The surgeon uses barb sutures to close the lumen, ensuring the tissue is securely held. Occasionally, the suture length may be insufficient for termination, highlighting the need for varied suture sizes.

The surgeon handles a large suture with needles on either side, preparing to cut it to a manageable size for endoscopic use. The suture is adjusted to avoid entanglement, ensuring ease of handling within the surgical field.

The surgeon reinforces the gastric bypass closure by taking additional suture bites with an endoscopic needle, ensuring no chance of leakage. The needle is passed through the loop to lock the suture, followed by a termination bite to secure the closure.

The surgeon tightens the barb suture by pulling with a needle holder, pressing the tissue with the open jaw to ensure it is locked. A knot is tied, followed by an additional suture bite on healthy tissue, avoiding termination on the intra-atomic side.
The surgeon tightens the suture further on healthy tissue using a square knot to terminate the stitch. A Sarjan knot is also an option due to the barb and loop configuration.

The surgeon completes one half of the knot using a loop technique, similar to open surgery, and tightens it. The needle is left aside as the loop is used to tighten the knot further. The second half of the knot is then completed and terminated using the same loop.
The surgeon cuts the suture and removes the needle from the surgical site, preparing to perform a leak test on the gastric bypass anastomosis.

An atraumatic intestine clamp holds the afferent and efferent loops a few centimeters below the anastomosis site. The anesthetist injects 150 mL of Nitalin blue dye, visibly distending the intestine to check for leaks at the suturing site. The anastomosis is inspected, confirming no leakage as the dye distends the area.

The surgeon checks for leaks at the anastomosis site, ensuring no complications. After releasing the grasp, the tissue collapses, and the phobias ring becomes visible. The surgical field is inspected for bleeding, confirming the completion of the mini gastric bypass procedure.
After the failure of the sleeve gastrectomy with phobias ring. Have a nice day. Thank you very much for your valuable time to watch this video. Bye.
